11	# SPI to WS2812 — Use Case for CLB Using the PIC16F13145 Microcontroller With MCC Melody
12	
13	This repository provides an MPLAB® X project for interfacing the Configurable Logic Block (CLB) and Serial Peripheral Interface (SPI) peripherals with a WS2812 LED matrix.
14	
15	The CLB peripheral is a collection of logic elements that can be programmed to perform a wide variety of digital logic functions. The logic function may be completely combinatorial, sequential or a combination of the two, enabling users to incorporate hardware-based custom logic into their applications.
16	
17	## Related Documentation
18	
19	More details and code examples on the PIC16F13145 can be found at the following links:
20	
21	- [PIC16F13145 Product Page](https://www.microchip.com/en-us/product/PIC16F13145?utm_source=GitHub&utm_medium=TextLink&utm_campaign=MCU8_MMTCha_PIC16F13145&utm_content=pic16f13145-spi-ws2812-mplab-mcc&utm_bu=MCU08)
22	- [PIC16F13145 Code Examples on Discover](https://mplab-discover.microchip.com/v2?dsl=PIC16F13145)
23	- [PIC16F13145 Code Examples on GitHub](https://github.com/microchip-pic-avr-examples/?q=PIC16F13145)
24	- [WS2818 Data Sheet](https://cdn-shop.adafruit.com/datasheets/WS2812.pdf)
25	
26	## Software Used
27	
28	- [MPLAB® X IDE v6.30 or newer](https://www.microchip.com/en-us/tools-resources/develop/mplab-x-ide?utm_source=GitHub&utm_medium=TextLink&utm_campaign=MCU8_MMTCha_PIC16F13145&utm_content=pic16f13145-spi-ws2812-mplab-mcc&utm_bu=MCU08) or [MPLAB® Tools for VS Code®](https://www.microchip.com/en-us/tools-resources/develop/mplab-tools-vs-code?utm_source=GitHub&utm_medium=TextLink&utm_campaign=MCU8_MMTCha_PIC16F13145&utm_content=pic16f13145-spi-ws2812-mplab-mcc&utm_bu=MCU08)
29	- [MPLAB® XC8 v3.10 or newer](https://www.microchip.com/en-us/tools-resources/develop/mplab-xc-compilers?utm_source=GitHub&utm_medium=TextLink&utm_campaign=MCU8_MMTCha_PIC16F13145&utm_content=pic16f13145-spi-ws2812-mplab-mcc&utm_bu=MCU08)
30	- [PIC16F1xxxx_DFP v1.29.444 or newer](https://packs.download.microchip.com/)

32	**Important:** The current version features an update to the CLB peripheral, which now includes the CLB Synthesizer Library. For migration details and required changes, refer to the [_Troubleshooting MCC Melody Configurable Logic Block (CLB) Projects Configured With CLB v1.x.x_](https://onlinedocs.microchip.com/oxy/GUID-9438FEC3-C80B-4328-8A8E-2531EDEE6155-en-US-1/index.html) migration guide documentation.

44	## Operation
45	
46	To program the Curiosity Nano board with this MPLAB X project, follow the steps provided in the [How to Program the Curiosity Nano Board](#how-to-program-the-curiosity-nano-board) chapter.<br><br>
47	
48	## Concept
49	
50	This example demonstrates the capabilities of the CLB, a Core Independent Peripheral (CIP), that can control and manipulate the transmitted data through the SPI. The CLB can transform SPI data into the equivalent WS2812 data. The figure below shows the implemented solution.
51	
52	<br><img src="images/mcc_clb_circuit.png" width="800">
53	
54	The Serial Data Out (SDO) and Serial Clock (SCK) signals define the application. The signals are inputs of the two off-sheet demux circuits, as shown in the image below. The first bit from the CLBSWIN register - `CLBSWIN[0]` - is used as the selector bit input for the same demux circuits.
55	
56	The `MSSP1_SDO`, `CLBSWIN0` and `MSSP1_SCK` pins are configured as input pins for CLB, `SDO` and `SWIN0` described as synchronized inputs. The `SCK` pin is configured as a positive edge detector, which will output only pulses. Those pins are also routed to different output pins, called `PPS_OUTx`, that can be set to log the signals with a logic analyzer for debugging.
57	
58	<br><img src="images/mcc_clb_demux_1x2.png" width="800">
59	
60	If the CLBSWIN0 bit is set, the SPI signals will be routed to the `SPI_to_WS2812` circuit, that is shown in the capture below, composed of a D Latch, a 3-bit counter with reset and one Look-Up Table (LUT). The output value of the 4-bit LUT is `0x7E0E`, used to manage the specific timings for the WS2812 LED matrix, which can be found in this [data sheet](https://cdn-shop.adafruit.com/datasheets/WS2812.pdf). The first three inputs of the LUT (A, B, C) are the outputs of the 3-bit counter, while the fourth input of the LUT is used to choose the right pattern for the matrix, so there are two sets of eight bits. In the green box the D value is `0`, meaning the "0 code" is chosen and described by three high periods and five low periods, and the "1 code" is described by six periods high and two periods low. Those periods represent the right timings mentioned in the data sheet to power up the LEDs in the desired pattern.
61	
62	<br><img src="images/mcc_clb_spi_to_ws2812.png" width="800">
63	
64	To power up an LED, a "1 code" signal must be transmitted, meaning a Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) signal with 0.7 μs ± 150 ns high and 0.6 μs ± 150 ns low periods. To power-off, a "0 code" signal will be transmitted, meaning a PWM signal with 0.35 μs ± 150 ns high and 0.8 μs ± 150 ns low periods. For this example, the "1 code" signal is described by six high signal cycles, `1` logic, and two low signal cycles, `0` logic, and the "0 code" is composed of three high signal cycles and seven low signal cycles. Each transmitted byte describes ten cycles. The D Latch with Enable is used as the fourth input of the LUT to select the needed sequence chart for the WS2812, if the bit from the SDO signal is `0` or `1`. For a better understanding of the above information, the diagram below presents the sequence charts and timings.
65	
66	<br><img src="images/sequence-timings.png" width="800">
67	
68	This 3-bit counter with reset is used to count up to eight values that describe each of the GRB pattern colors. Inverted SPI clock pulses reset the counter. The counter is enabled when all the outputs are high by a 3-AND gate, so the counter is kept in Reset state until the next pulse is coming. In this configuration, the counter works only when an enable pulse is met and stops and resets when the last value, 7, appears. The output values represent the input of the next LUT necessary for setting the "0 code" and "1 code". The figure below presents all the gates and pins needed to simulate the hardware counter.
69	
70	<br><img src="images/mcc_clb_counter_3bit_reset.png" width="800">
71	
72	One necessary period for the WS2812 "0/1 code" is approximately 1.25 μs, and depends from a manufacturer to another. To have the desired period output, set the System Clock to 32 MHz, the CLB clock as the System Clock divided by four, and the SPI clock to 800 kHz. These settings will let the entire CLB circuit generate an output of the good timings for the WS2812 LED matrix. For other types of neopixels, the specified clock values must be changed manually to get the desired timings.
73	
74	For a better understanding of the circuit, debugging output pins are used and read with a logic analyzer to display the digital signals.
75	
76	<br><img src="images/logic_signals.PNG" width="1000">
77	
78	When the CLBSWIN0 is `0`, the SDO and SCK signals from the demux circuits are displayed.
79	
80	<br><img src="images/logic_swin_0_spi_pattern.png" width="1000">
81	
82	When the CLBSWIN1 is set, the SPI_to_WS2812 output that is connected next to the WS2812 matrix is displayed. The "0 code" sequence chart is made out of the first three pairs of timing markers: P0-T0H cycle, P1-T0L cycle and P2 (the joining between P0 and P1). The next three pairs of timing markers, P3-T1H cycle, P4-T1L cycle and P5 (the joining between P3 and P4), represent the "1 code" sequence chart.
83	
84	<br><img src="images/logic_swin_1_ws2812_pattern.png" width="1000">
85	
86	## Setup
87	
88	The following peripheral and clock configurations are set up using the MPLAB Code Configurator (MCC) Melody for the PIC16F13145:
89	
90	1. Configuration Bits:
91	   - CONFIG1:
92	     - External Oscillator mode selection bits: Oscillator not enabled
93	     - Power-up default value for COSC bits: HFINTOSC (1 MHz)
94	       <br><img src="images/mcc_config_bits_1.png" width="400">
95	   - CONFIG2:
96	     - Brown-out reset enable bits: Brown-out reset disabled
97	       <br><img src="images/mcc_config_bits_2.png" width="400">
98	   - CONFIG3:
99	     - WDT operating mode: WDT Disabled, SEN is ignored
100	       <br><img src="images/mcc_config_bits_3.png" width="400">
101	
102	2. Clock Control:
103	   - Clock Source: HFINTOSC
104	   - HF Internal Clock: 32_MHz
105	   - Clock Divider: 1
106	     <br><img src="images/mcc_clock_control.png" width="400">
107	
108	3. CLB Synthesizer Library:
109	   - Enable CLB: Enabled
110	   - Clock Selection: HFINTOSC
111	   - Clock Divider: Divide clock source by 4
112	     <br><img src="images/mcc_clb.png" width="400">
113	
114	4. MSSP1 (SPI):
115	   - Serial Protocol: SPI
116	     - Mode: Host
117	     - SPI Mode: SPI Mode 1
118	     - Input Data Sampled At: Middle
119	     - Clock Source Selection: FOSC/4_SSPxADD
120	     - SPI Clock Frequency (Hz): 800000 (800 kHz)
121	     - Interrupt Driven: Disabled
122	
123	       <img src="images/mcc_mssp.png" width="400">
124	
125	5. CRC:
126	   - Auto-configured by CLB
127	
128	6. Pin Grid View:
129	   - CLBPPSOUT0: RB4 (CLBSWIN0)
130	   - CLBPPSOUT1: RB5 (SDO)
131	   - CLBPPSOUT2: RB6 (SCK)
132	   - CLBPPSOUT7: RB7 (SPI_to_WS2812 out)
133	     <br><img src="images/mcc_pin_grid_view.png" width="600">
134	
135	<br>
136	
137	## Demo
138	
139	Two patterns are saved in the `image.h` header file called `imageR` and `imageG` variables. Those variables help display the **CLB** acronym in two different colors, red and green, as shown in the demo below. Only two wires are needed between the microcontroller and the WS2812 - the output pin from the CLB (RB7) and the ground (GND).
140	
141	<br><img src="images/demo.gif" width="1000">
142	
143	**Note:** The WS2812 matrix must be externally powered up due to a higher power consumption.
144	
145	<br>
146	
147	## Summary
148	
149	This example demonstrates the capabilities of the CLB, a CIP, that controls and manipulates the transmitted data through the SPI to power up a WS2812 matrix in a desired pattern.
150	
151	<br>
152	
153	## How to Program the Curiosity Nano Board
154	
155	This chapter demonstrates how to use the MPLAB X IDE to program a PIC® device with an Example_Project.X. This is applicable to other projects.
156	
157	1.  Connect the board to the PC.
158	
159	2.  Open the `Example_Project.X` project in MPLAB X IDE.
160	
161	3.  Set the `Example_Project.X` project as main project.
162	    <br>Right click the project in the **Projects** tab and click **Set as Main Project**.
163	    <br><img src="images/Program_Set_as_Main_Project.png" width="600">
164	
165	4.  Clean and build the `Example_Project.X` project.
166	    <br>Right click the `Example_Project.X` project and select **Clean and Build**.
167	    <br><img src="images/Program_Clean_and_Build.png" width="600">
168	
169	5.  Select **PICxxxxx Curiosity Nano** in the Connected Hardware Tool section of the project settings:
170	    <br>Right click the project and click **Properties**.
171	    <br>Click the arrow under the Connected Hardware Tool.
172	    <br>Select **PICxxxxx Curiosity Nano** (click the **SN**), click **Apply** and then click **OK**:
173	    <br><img src="images/Program_Tool_Selection.png" width="600">
174	
175	6.  Program the project to the board.
176	    <br>Right click the project and click **Make and Program Device**.
177	    <br><img src="images/Program_Make_and_Program_Device.png" width="600">
178	
179	<br>

**Current Hardware/Firmware:**
- PIC16F13145 Curiosity Nano board (has USB connection for programming/power)
- 8x32 WS2812 LED matrix (256 total LEDs)
- SPI communication to LEDs via CLB (Configurable Logic Block)
- Currently displays pre-programmed patterns (imageR, imageG from image.h)

**To build a GUI, the user needs:**
1. A way to communicate with the MCU from a PC
2. USB serial communication (the Curiosity Nano board can provide this through its debug USB port)
3. A GUI on the PC to send LED commands to the MCU
4. Modified firmware to receive serial commands and control the LEDs

Now let me provide tool recommendations based on what would work best for this use case. The key factors are:
- Need bidirectional communication between PC and MCU
- 8x32 LED matrix = potentially complex UI to visualize
- Common options: Python (PyQt/PySimpleGUI), C#/.NET (WinForms/WPF), web-based (Electron/web technologies), or platform-specific tools

Let me give informed recommendations without building yet.
</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 2026-05-06 19:08:28Z)**_

Based on your PIC16F13145 WS2812 LED project, here are the best GUI tools for controlling the LEDs from a PC:

## Communication Layer
First, you'll need serial communication. Your Curiosity Nano has a USB debug port that provides a virtual COM port—you'd add a serial receive routine to the firmware to listen for LED commands.

## GUI Options (ranked by suitability):

**1. Python + PyQt/PySimpleGUI** ✓ Best for rapid prototyping
- **Pros**: Cross-platform, great for embedded dev, can visualize 8x32 grid easily, simple serial communication via `pyserial`
- **Cons**: Requires Python runtime on user's machine
- **Best for**: Developer-focused tool, iterating on LED patterns quickly

**2. Electron + React/Vue** ✓ Best for polished desktop app
- **Pros**: Modern UI, easy to build grid visualizers, distributable as standalone app (.exe)
- **Cons**: Heavier resource footprint, more setup required
- **Best for**: Sharing with non-technical users, professional appearance

**3. C# WinForms/WPF** ✓ Best for Windows-only
- **Pros**: Native Windows performance, straightforward serial communication
- **Cons**: Windows-only, older UI toolkit (WinForms)
- **Best for**: Enterprise/corporate use

**4. Web-based (HTML/JS + Node backend)** ✓ Best for flexibility
- **Pros**: Works on any browser/OS, easy to share over network
- **Cons**: More complex setup, needs backend server
- **Best for**: Future expansion to control LEDs remotely

## My recommendation:
**Start with Python + PySimpleGUI** (simplest) or **Python + PyQt5** (more polished). Both let you:
- Visualize the 8x32 grid as clickable cells
- Pick colors easily
- See real-time updates from the MCU
- Prototype patterns quickly before refining

What's your preference: quick prototyping, standalone app distribution, or web-based access?

The user wants me to modify the existing project to control only 8 WS2812 LEDs instead of the 8x32 LED matrix. 

Let me first understand what needs to change:
1. The current project is set up for an 8x32 matrix (256 LEDs total)
2. They want to reduce it to just 8 LEDs
3. The main changes would be in:
   - main.c: NUMBER_OF_ROWS, NUMBER_OF_COLUMNS, and the image data
   - image.h: The image data for the LEDs
   - Potentially the CLB configuration if needed, but probably not since it's just about the number of LEDs

Let me read the image.h file to see what we're working with, then I can make the modifications.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user wants me to create a serial protocol for controlling 8 tri-color LEDs via UART. The interface will be unidirectional (GUI to MCU). 

I should design a protocol that:
1. Allows control of individual LEDs
2. Allows setting RGB colors for each LED
3. Is simple and efficient
4. Is extensible for future features

Let me think about good protocol designs:

Option 1: Simple ASCII-based
- Each LED can be addressed individually
- Format: `L<LED_NUMBER>:<R>,<G>,<B>\n`
- Example: `L0:255,0,0\n` sets LED 0 to red

Option 2: Binary protocol
- More efficient but harder to debug
- Header byte, LED number, RGB values, checksum

Option 3: Command-based
- Different commands for different operations
- `SET` command for setting individual LEDs
- `UPDATE` command to apply changes

I think a good approach would be:
1. ASCII-based for simplicity and debuggability
2. Simple command structure
3. Maybe support setting all LEDs at once
4. Support setting individual LEDs
5. Some kind of acknowledgment/response for verification

Let me design a protocol:

**Simple Protocol:**
- Command format: `@<CMD><LED_NUM>:<R>,<G>,<B>\n`
- Where CMD could be:
  - `S` = Set individual LED
  - `A` = Set all LEDs to same color
  - `R` = Reset/clear all LEDs
  - `U` = Update display (apply changes)

Examples:
- `@S0:255,0,0\n` - Set LED 0 to red
- `@A:255,0,0\n` - Set all LEDs to red
- `@R\n` - Reset all LEDs to off
- `@U\n` - Update/commit changes

Actually, the user said the interface is unidirectional (GUI to MCU), so we don't need responses necessarily. 

Even though it's one-way communication, adding acknowledgments would still be useful for error checking. I'll structure this with a clear frame format using a start byte, command, data payload, checksum, and end byte. For ASCII simplicity, I could use `@` as the start marker and `\n` as the terminator. The command set would include individual LED control (`S<N>:<R>,<G>,<B>`), bulk operations like setting all LEDs (`A:<R>,<G>,<B>`), clearing everything (`C`), and an update command to apply pending changes.

Actually, a more practical approach might be sending all 24 bytes at once—three bytes per LED across eight LEDs—with a binary frame wrapper. But ASCII is more debuggable, so I could instead send comma-separated RGB values for each LED in sequence, or use individual commands for each one. The key is balancing simplicity with flexibility. I want something that's straightforward to parse on the microcontroller, human-readable during development, and extensible if I need to add features later. Adding a checksum for validation would catch transmission errors without being overly complex.

The protocol I'm settling on uses `@` as a start marker with command types: `@L<N>:<R>,<G>,<B>` for individual LEDs, `@A:<R>,<G>,<B>` to set all eight at once, `@C` to clear everything, and `@U` to apply changes if I implement buffering. I'd also add `@Q` for querying status in case I need bidirectional communication later. This gives me a clean, extensible foundation. Now I need to implement this with a header file containing the protocol constants and a parser function that handles incoming UART data, then integrate it into the existing code.
</details></think>

Now let me compile a comprehensive search report based on all the information I've gathered:

## CODEBASE SEARCH REPORT: PIC16F13145 SPI-to-WS2812 MPLAB MCC Project

### PROJECT OVERVIEW
**Location:** `/c/microchip/mchp_fw/PIC16F13145_CNANO/firmware/pic16f13145-spi-ws2812-mplab-mcc/pic16f13145_spi_to_ws2812_mcc.X/`

**Purpose:** This is a Microchip MCC-generated firmware project that controls WS2812 RGB LEDs via SPI protocol, with UART communication support.

---

### 1. UART/SERIAL COMMUNICATION SETUP AND FUNCTIONS

**UART Driver Files:**
- **Header:** `/mcc_generated_files/uart/eusart1.h`
- **Implementation:** `/mcc_generated_files/uart/src/eusart1.c`

**Key UART Functions:**

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `EUSART1_Initialize()` | Initialize EUSART1 with configured parameters (9600 baud, 8-bit, BRGL=0x44, BRGH=0x0) |
| `EUSART1_Enable()` / `EUSART1_Disable()` | Enable/disable UART module |
| `EUSART1_Read()` | Read byte from RX buffer |
| `EUSART1_Write(uint8_t txData)` | Write byte to TX buffer |
| `EUSART1_IsRxReady()` | Check if RX data available |
| `EUSART1_IsTxReady()` | Check if TX ready to accept data |
| `EUSART1_TransmitEnable()` / `EUSART1_TransmitDisable()` | TX control |
| `EUSART1_ReceiveEnable()` / `EUSART1_ReceiveDisable()` | RX control |
| `EUSART1_ReceiveISR()` | Receive Interrupt Service Routine |
| `EUSART1_TransmitISR()` | Transmit Interrupt Service Routine |

**Error Handling:**
- `EUSART1_ErrorGet()` - Returns error status (parity, framing, overrun)
- `EUSART1_FramingErrorCallbackRegister()` - Register framing error callback
- `EUSART1_OverrunErrorCallbackRegister()` - Register overrun error callback

**Interrupt Callbacks:**
- `EUSART1_RxCompleteCallbackRegister()` - Register RX complete callback
- `EUSART1_TxCompleteCallbackRegister()` - Register TX complete callback

---

### 2. CIRCULAR/RING BUFFER IMPLEMENTATION

**Found in:** `/mcc_generated_files/uart/src/eusart1.c` (lines 43-66)

**Buffer Configuration:**
```
TX Buffer Size: 8 bytes (2^n format)
TX Buffer Mask: 0x07 (for power-of-2 indexing)
RX Buffer Size: 8 bytes (2^n format)
RX Buffer Mask: 0x07
```

**Buffer Variables:**
- `eusart1TxHead` - TX write pointer
- `eusart1TxTail` - TX read pointer
- `eusart1TxBufferRemaining` - Available TX space
- `eusart1RxHead` - RX write pointer
- `eusart1RxTail` - RX read pointer
- `eusart1RxCount` - Bytes available in RX
- `eusart1RxStatusBuffer[8]` - Error status for each RX byte

**Buffer Mechanics:**
- Uses power-of-2 sizing with bit-mask (AND with BUFFER_MASK) for wrap-around
- Double-buffered with head/tail pointers
- RX overflow detection at line 305-308 (buffer full check)
- TX buffer remaining tracked to prevent overflow (line 330-338)

---

### 3. INTERRUPT HANDLERS AND SYSTEM INITIALIZATION

**Interrupt Management Files:**
- **Header:** `/mcc_generated_files/system/interrupt.h`
- **Implementation:** `/mcc_generated_files/system/src/interrupt.c`

**Main Interrupt Handler:**
- `INTERRUPT_InterruptManager()` - Central ISR dispatcher (lines 59-81)
  - Checks `PIE4bits.TX1IE` and `PIE4bits.RC1IE` enable flags
  - Routes to `EUSART1_TxInterruptHandler()` or `EUSART1_RxInterruptHandler()`

**Interrupt Initialization:**
- `INTERRUPT_Initialize()` - Called from system initialization
- Enables peripheral interrupt edge detection (rising edge for INT pin)

**External Interrupt Support:**
- `INT_ISR()` - External interrupt handler
- `INT_SetInterruptHandler()` - Dynamic handler registration
- `INT_CallBack()` - Callback mechanism

---

### 4. MAIN APPLICATION STRUCTURE

**Main Entry Point:** `/main.c` (lines 47-68)

**Application Flow:**
1. `SYSTEM_Initialize()` - Initialize all system modules
2. Define LED configuration (8 LEDs with RGB values)
3. Enable CLB (Configurable Logic Block): `CLBSWINLbits.CLBSWIN0 = 1`
4. Open SPI interface: `SPI1_Open(MSSP1_DEFAULT)`
5. Send LED data via SPI: `SPI1_BufferWrite((uint8_t *)leds, RGB_SIZE)`
6. Close SPI: `SPI1_Close()`
7. Infinite loop: `while(1);`

**LED Data Structure:**
```c
typedef struct {
    uint8_t green;
    uint8_t red;
    uint8_t blue;
} rgb_t;

#define NUMBER_OF_LEDS 8
#define RGB_SIZE (NUMBER_OF_LEDS * 3)  // 24 bytes
```

---

### 5. SYSTEM MODULE ORGANIZATION

**System Header:** `/mcc_generated_files/system/system.h`
**System Implementation:** `/mcc_generated_files/system/src/system.c`

**Initialization Sequence (SYSTEM_Initialize()):**
1. `CLOCK_Initialize()` - Clock configuration
2. `CLB1_Initialize()` - Configurable Logic Block setup
3. `PIN_MANAGER_Initialize()` - GPIO configuration
4. `SPI1_Initialize()` - SPI module setup
5. `CRC_Initialize()` - CRC module setup
6. `EUSART1_Initialize()` - UART setup
7. `INTERRUPT_Initialize()` - Interrupt configuration

---

### 6. FILE ORGANIZATION IN MCC_GENERATED_FILES

**Directory Structure:**

```
mcc_generated_files/
├── clb/
│   ├── clb1.h
│   └── src/clb1.c              (Configurable Logic Block driver)
├── crc/
│   ├── crc.h
│   └── src/crc.c               (CRC calculator)
├── spi/
│   ├── mssp1.h                 (SPI1 driver header)
│   ├── spi_interface.h          (Generic SPI interface)
│   ├── spi_polling_types.h      (SPI types for polling mode)
│   └── src/mssp1.c              (SPI1 driver implementation)
├── system/
│   ├── system.h                (Main system header)
│   ├── clock.h                 (Clock configuration)
│   ├── config_bits.h           (PIC16F13145 config bits)
│   ├── interrupt.h             (Interrupt management)
│   ├── pins.h                  (GPIO definitions)
│   └── src/
│       ├── system.c
│       ├── clock.c
│       ├── config_bits.c
│       ├── interrupt.c
│       └── pins.c
└── uart/
    ├── eusart1.h               (UART driver header)
    └── src/eusart1.c           (UART implementation with buffers)
```

---

### 7. SPI MODULE - DATA TRANSFER INTERFACE

**SPI Interface Files:**
- **Generic Interface:** `/mcc_generated_files/spi/spi_interface.h`
- **SPI1 Header:** `/mcc_generated_files/spi/mssp1.h`
- **SPI1 Implementation:** `/mcc_generated_files/spi/src/mssp1.c`

**Key SPI Functions:**
- `SPI1_Initialize()` / `SPI1_Deinitialize()`
- `SPI1_Open(uint8_t spiConfigIndex)` - Enable with configuration
- `SPI1_Close()` - Disable SPI module
- `SPI1_BufferWrite(void *bufferData, size_t bufferSize)` - Send buffer (used for LED data)
- `SPI1_BufferRead()` - Receive buffer
- `SPI1_ByteWrite()` / `SPI1_ByteRead()` - Single byte operations

**SPI Configuration:**
- Uses MSSP1 (Master Synchronous Serial Port) module
- Single configuration: `MSSP1_DEFAULT`
- Polling-based (non-interrupt) data transfer

---

### 8. GPIO AND PIN MANAGEMENT

**Pins Header:** `/mcc_generated_files/system/pins.h`

**Configured I/O Pins:**
- `IO_RB4` - Port B4 GPIO
- `IO_RB5` - Port B5 GPIO (SPI SCK likely)
- `IO_RB6` - Port B6 GPIO (SPI SDO likely)
- `IO_RB7` - Port B7 GPIO (SPI SDI likely)
- `IO_RC4` - Port C4 GPIO (UART RX likely)
- `IO_RC5` - Port C5 GPIO (UART TX likely)

**Pin Macros Provided:**
- `_SetHigh()` / `_SetLow()` - Digital output control
- `_SetDigitalInput()` / `_SetDigitalOutput()` - Direction control
- `_GetValue()` - Read pin state
- `_SetAnalogMode()` / `_SetDigitalMode()` - Mode selection
- `_SetPullup()` / `_ResetPullup()` - Pull-up control

---

### 9. ADDITIONAL MODULES

**CLB (Configurable Logic Block):**
- File: `/mcc_generated_files/clb/clb1.h`
- Functions: `CLB1_Initialize()`, `CLB1_Enable()`, `CLB1_SWIN_Write*()`, `CLB1_SWIN_Read*()`
- Used for timing control of WS2812 protocol

**CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check):**
- File: `/mcc_generated_files/crc/crc.h`
- Functions: `CRC_Initialize()`, `CRC_WriteData()`, `CRC_GetCalculatedResult()`
- Scanner mode for bulk memory CRC verification

---

### 10. DATA STRUCTURES AND PROTOCOLS

**UART Error Status Structure (eusart1_status_t):**
```c
typedef union {
    struct {
        uint8_t perr : 1;      // Parity error
        uint8_t ferr : 1;      // Framing error
        uint8_t oerr : 1;      // Overrun error
        uint8_t reserved : 5;
    };
    size_t status;             // Full status byte
} eusart1_status_t;
```

**WS2812 LED Data (image.h):**
- Predefined LED patterns in `imageR[]`, `imageG[]` arrays
- IMAGE_SIZE: 24 bytes (8 LEDs × 3 channels)

---

### 11. NAMING CONVENTIONS AND PATTERNS

**File Organization:**
- MCC-generated files: `/mcc_generated_files/[module]/` + `/src/` subdirectory
- Headers in module directory, implementations in `src/` subdirectory

**Naming Pattern:**
- UART: `eusart1` (Enhanced UART 1)
- SPI: `mssp1` (Master Synchronous Serial Port 1)
- CLB: `clb1` (Configurable Logic Block 1)
- CRC: `crc`

**Function Naming:**
- `[MODULE]_Initialize()` - Initialization
- `[MODULE]_Enable()` / `[MODULE]_Disable()` - Module control
- `[MODULE]_Read()` / `[MODULE]_Write()` - Data transfer
- `[MODULE]_ISR()` - Interrupt service routine
- `[MODULE]_CallbackRegister()` - Dynamic callback registration

---

### 12. PROJECT FILES SUMMARY

| File Path | Lines | Purpose |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| `main.c` | 68 | Application entry point, LED control logic |
| `image.h` | 48 | Predefined LED pattern data |
| `mcc_generated_files/uart/eusart1.h` | 348 | UART API header |
| `mcc_generated_files/uart/src/eusart1.c` | 413 | UART driver (with 8-byte ring buffers) |
| `mcc_generated_files/spi/mssp1.h` | 163 | SPI API header |
| `mcc_generated_files/spi/src/mssp1.c` | 100+ | SPI driver |
| `mcc_generated_files/system/system.h` | 61 | System coordination |
| `mcc_generated_files/system/src/interrupt.c` | 111 | Interrupt dispatcher |
| `mcc_generated_files/clb/clb1.h` | 128 | CLB driver (102-byte bitstream) |
| `mcc_generated_files/crc/crc.h` | 133 | CRC calculator |

---

### KEY FINDINGS SUMMARY

1. **UART is fully implemented** with 8-byte circular buffers for both TX and RX, interrupt-driven operation, and error handling callbacks
2. **Ring buffers use power-of-2 sizing** (8 bytes) with bit-mask wrap-around for efficiency
3. **System uses centralized interrupt dispatcher** that routes TX1 and RC1 interrupts to UART ISR handlers
4. **Application is minimal** - just a single-shot LED update via SPI with no ongoing protocol handling
5. **No complex data parsing** is currently implemented - the application sends static LED patterns
6. **CLB module is used** for WS2812 timing (requires specific timing protocol for addressable LEDs)
7. **All modules follow MCC naming conventions** with Initialize/Enable/Disable/Read/Write/ISR patterns
8. **Callback-based architecture** allows runtime handler registration for UART error and completion events

44	#define EUSART1_TX_BUFFER_MASK (EUSART1_TX_BUFFER_SIZE - 1U) 
45	
46	#define EUSART1_RX_BUFFER_SIZE (8U) //buffer size should be 2^n
47	#define EUSART1_RX_BUFFER_MASK (EUSART1_RX_BUFFER_SIZE - 1U)

53	static volatile uint8_t eusart1TxTail = 0;
54	static volatile uint8_t eusart1TxBufferRemaining;
55	static volatile uint8_t eusart1TxBuffer[EUSART1_TX_BUFFER_SIZE];
56

58	static volatile uint8_t eusart1RxTail = 0;
59	static volatile uint8_t eusart1RxCount;
60	static volatile uint8_t eusart1RxBuffer[EUSART1_RX_BUFFER_SIZE];
61	/**
62	 * @misradeviation{@advisory,19.2}
63	 * The UART error status necessitates checking the bitfield and accessing the status within the group byte therefore the use of a union is essential.
64	 */
65	 /* cppcheck-suppress misra-c2012-19.2 */
66	static volatile eusart1_status_t eusart1RxStatusBuffer[EUSART1_RX_BUFFER_SIZE];
67

69	 * @misradeviation{@advisory,19.2}
70	 * The UART error status necessitates checking the bitfield and accessing the status within the group byte therefore the use of a union is essential.
71	 */
72	 /* cppcheck-suppress misra-c2012-19.2 */
73	static volatile eusart1_status_t eusart1RxLastError;

76	  Section: EUSART1 APIs
77	*/
78	
79	void (*EUSART1_TxInterruptHandler)(void);
80	/* cppcheck-suppress misra-c2012-8.9 */

82	
83	void (*EUSART1_RxInterruptHandler)(void);
84	static void (*EUSART1_RxCompleteInterruptHandler)(void) = NULL;
85	
86	static void (*EUSART1_FramingErrorHandler)(void) = NULL;
87	static void (*EUSART1_OverrunErrorHandler)(void) = NULL;
88

90	static void EUSART1_DefaultOverrunErrorCallback(void);

97	  Section: EUSART1  APIs

99	
100	void EUSART1_Initialize(void)
101	{
102	    PIE4bits.RC1IE = 0;   
103	     EUSART1_RxInterruptHandler = EUSART1_ReceiveISR;   
104	
105	    PIE4bits.TX1IE = 0; 
106	    EUSART1_TxInterruptHandler = EUSART1_TransmitISR; 
107	
108	    // Set the EUSART1 module to the options selected in the user interface.
109	
110	    //ABDEN disabled; WUE disabled; BRG16 16bit_generator; SCKP Non-Inverted; 
111	    BAUD1CON = 0x48; 
112	    //ADDEN disabled; CREN enabled; SREN disabled; RX9 8-bit; SPEN enabled; 
113	    RC1STA = 0x90; 
114	    //TX9D 0x0; BRGH hi_speed; SENDB sync_break_complete; SYNC asynchronous; TXEN enabled; TX9 8-bit; CSRC client; 
115	    TX1STA = 0x26; 
116	    //SPBRGL 68; 
117	    SP1BRGL = 0x44; 
118	    //SPBRGH 0; 
119	    SP1BRGH = 0x0; 
120	
121	    EUSART1_FramingErrorCallbackRegister(EUSART1_DefaultFramingErrorCallback);
122	    EUSART1_OverrunErrorCallbackRegister(EUSART1_DefaultOverrunErrorCallback);
123	    eusart1RxLastError.status = 0;  
124	
125	    eusart1TxHead = 0;
126	    eusart1TxTail = 0;
127	    eusart1TxBufferRemaining = sizeof(eusart1TxBuffer);
128	
129	    eusart1RxHead = 0;
130	    eusart1RxTail = 0;
131	    eusart1RxCount = 0;
132	
133	    PIE4bits.RC1IE = 1; 
134	}
135	
136	void EUSART1_Deinitialize(void)
137	{
138	    PIE4bits.RC1IE = 0;    
139	    PIE4bits.TX1IE = 0; 
140	    BAUD1CON = 0x00;
141	    RC1STA = 0x00;
142	    TX1STA = 0x00;
143	    SP1BRGL = 0x00;
144	    SP1BRGH = 0x00;
145	}
146	
147	void EUSART1_Enable(void)
148	{
149	    RC1STAbits.SPEN = 1;
150	
151	}
152	
153	void EUSART1_Disable(void)
154	{
155	    RC1STAbits.SPEN = 0;
156	}
157	
158

161	    TX1STAbits.TXEN = 1;
162	}

165	{
166	    TX1STAbits.TXEN = 0;
167	}

171	    RC1STAbits.CREN = 1;
172	}
173

175	{
176	    RC1STAbits.CREN = 0;
177	}

181	    TX1STAbits.SENDB = 1;
182	}
183

185	{
186	    TX1STAbits.SENDB = 0;
187	}
188	
189	void EUSART1_AutoBaudSet(bool enable)
190	{
191	    if(enable)
192	    {
193	        BAUD1CONbits.ABDEN = 1;
194	    }
195	    else
196	    {
197	       BAUD1CONbits.ABDEN = 0; 
198	    }
199	}
200	
201	bool EUSART1_AutoBaudQuery(void)
202	{
203	return (bool)(!BAUD1CONbits.ABDEN);
204	}
205

207	{
208	    return (bool)BAUD1CONbits.ABDOVF; 
209	}
210	
211	void EUSART1_AutoBaudDetectOverflowReset(void)
212	{
213	    BAUD1CONbits.ABDOVF = 0; 
214	}

217	{
218	    PIE4bits.TX1IE = 1;
219	
220	}
221

223	{ 
224	    PIE4bits.TX1IE = 0; 
225	}

230	}

232	{
233	    PIE4bits.RC1IE = 0; 
234	}
235	
236	bool EUSART1_IsRxReady(void)
237	{
238	    return (eusart1RxCount ? true : false);
239	}
240	
241	bool EUSART1_IsTxReady(void)
242	{
243	    return (eusart1TxBufferRemaining ? true : false);
244	}
245	
246	bool EUSART1_IsTxDone(void)
247	{
248	    return TX1STAbits.TRMT;
249	}
250	
251	size_t EUSART1_ErrorGet(void)
252	{
253	    eusart1RxLastError.status = eusart1RxStatusBuffer[(eusart1RxTail) & EUSART1_RX_BUFFER_MASK].status;
254	    return eusart1RxLastError.status;
255	}
256	
257	uint8_t EUSART1_Read(void)
258	{
259	    uint8_t readValue  = 0;
260	    uint8_t tempRxTail;
261	    
262	    readValue = eusart1RxBuffer[eusart1RxTail];
263	
264	    tempRxTail = (eusart1RxTail + 1U) & EUSART1_RX_BUFFER_MASK; // Buffer size of RX should be in the 2^n
265	    
266	    eusart1RxTail = tempRxTail;
267	
268	    PIE4bits.RC1IE = 0; 
269	    if(0U != eusart1RxCount)
270	    {
271	        eusart1RxCount--;
272	    }
273	    PIE4bits.RC1IE = 1;
274	    return readValue;
275	}
276	
277	void EUSART1_ReceiveISR(void)
278	{
279	    uint8_t regValue;
280	    uint8_t tempRxHead;
281	
282	    // use this default receive interrupt handler code
283	    eusart1RxStatusBuffer[eusart1RxHead].status = 0;
284	
285	    if(true == RC1STAbits.OERR)
286	    {
287	        eusart1RxStatusBuffer[eusart1RxHead].oerr = 1;
288	        if(NULL != EUSART1_OverrunErrorHandler)
289	        {
290	            EUSART1_OverrunErrorHandler();
291	        }   
292	    }   
293	    if(true == RC1STAbits.FERR)
294	    {
295	        eusart1RxStatusBuffer[eusart1RxHead].ferr = 1;
296	        if(NULL != EUSART1_FramingErrorHandler)
297	        {
298	            EUSART1_FramingErrorHandler();
299	        }   
300	    } 
301	    
302	    regValue = RC1REG;
303	    
304	    tempRxHead = (eusart1RxHead + 1U) & EUSART1_RX_BUFFER_MASK;// Buffer size of RX should be in the 2^n
305	    if (tempRxHead == eusart1RxTail) 
306	    {
307			// ERROR! Receive buffer overflow 
308		} 
309	    else
310	    {
311	        eusart1RxBuffer[eusart1RxHead] = regValue;
312			eusart1RxHead = tempRxHead;
313			eusart1RxCount++;
314		}   
315	
316	    if(NULL != EUSART1_RxCompleteInterruptHandler)
317	    {
318	        (*EUSART1_RxCompleteInterruptHandler)();
319	    } 
320	}
321	
322	void EUSART1_Write(uint8_t txData)
323	{
324	    uint8_t tempTxHead;
325	    
326	    if(0 == PIE4bits.TX1IE)
327	    {
328	        TX1REG = txData;
329	    }
330	    else if(0U < eusart1TxBufferRemaining) // check if at least one byte place is available in TX buffer
331	    {
332	       eusart1TxBuffer[eusart1TxHead] = txData;
333	       tempTxHead = (eusart1TxHead + 1U) & EUSART1_TX_BUFFER_MASK;
334	       
335	       eusart1TxHead = tempTxHead;
336	       PIE4bits.TX1IE = 0; //Critical value decrement
337	       eusart1TxBufferRemaining--; // one less byte remaining in TX buffer
338	    }
339	    else
340	    {
341	        //overflow condition; eusart1TxBufferRemaining is 0 means TX buffer is full
342	    }
343	    PIE4bits.TX1IE = 1;
344	}
345	
346	void EUSART1_TransmitISR(void)
347	{
348	    uint8_t tempTxTail;
349	    // use this default transmit interrupt handler code
350	    if(sizeof(eusart1TxBuffer) > eusart1TxBufferRemaining) // check if all data is transmitted
351	    {
352	       TX1REG = eusart1TxBuffer[eusart1TxTail];
353	       tempTxTail = (eusart1TxTail + 1U) & EUSART1_TX_BUFFER_MASK;
354	       
355	       eusart1TxTail = tempTxTail;
356	       eusart1TxBufferRemaining++; // one byte sent, so 1 more byte place is available in TX buffer
357	    }
358	    else
359	    {
360	        PIE4bits.TX1IE = 0;
361	    }
362	
363	    if(NULL != EUSART1_TxCompleteInterruptHandler)
364	    {
365	        (*EUSART1_TxCompleteInterruptHandler)();
366	    }
367	}
368	
369	static void EUSART1_DefaultFramingErrorCallback(void)
370	{
371	    
372	}
373	
374	static void EUSART1_DefaultOverrunErrorCallback(void)
375	{
376	    //Continuous Receive must be cleared to clear Overrun Error else Rx will not receive upcoming bytes
377	    RC1STAbits.CREN = 0;
378	    RC1STAbits.CREN = 1;
379	}
380	
381	void EUSART1_FramingErrorCallbackRegister(void (* callbackHandler)(void))
382	{
383	    if(NULL != callbackHandler)
384	    {
385	        EUSART1_FramingErrorHandler = callbackHandler;
386	    }
387	}
388	
389	void EUSART1_OverrunErrorCallbackRegister(void (* callbackHandler)(void))
390	{
391	    if(NULL != callbackHandler)
392	    {
393	        EUSART1_OverrunErrorHandler = callbackHandler;
394	    }    
395	}
396

398	{
399	    if(NULL != callbackHandler)
400	    {
401	       EUSART1_RxCompleteInterruptHandler = callbackHandler; 
402	    }   
403	}

38	/**
39	 * @ingroup interrupt

125	 */
126	#define EXT_INT_fallingEdgeSet()          (INTCONbits.INTEDG = 0)

59	void __interrupt() INTERRUPT_InterruptManager (void)

76	    }

80	    }
81	}

40	    .Deinitialize = SPI1_Deinitialize,

42	    .Close = SPI1_Close,

51	    .RxCompleteCallbackRegister = NULL,
52	    .TxCompleteCallbackRegister = NULL

55	static const spi_configuration_t spi1_configuration[] = {
56	    { 0x24, 0xA, 0x10, 0x9 }
57	};
58	
59	void SPI1_Initialize(void)
60	{
61	    // Return mssp1 registers to reset state
62	    PIE5bits.SSP1IE  = 0U;
63	    PIR5bits.SSP1IF    = 0U;
64	
65	    SSP1STAT = (uint8_t)0x00;
66	    SSP1CON1 = (uint8_t)0x00;
67	    SSP1CON3 = (uint8_t)0x00;
68	    SSP1ADD  = (uint8_t)0x00;
69	}
70	
71	void SPI1_Deinitialize(void)
72	{
73	    // Return mssp1 registers to reset state
74	    SSP1STAT = (uint8_t)0x00;
75	    SSP1CON1 = (uint8_t)0x00;
76	    SSP1CON3 = (uint8_t)0x00;
77	    SSP1ADD  = (uint8_t)0x00;
78	}
79	
80	bool SPI1_Open(uint8_t spiConfigIndex)
81	{
82	    bool returnValue = false;
83	    if (SSP1CON1bits.SSPEN == 0U)
84	    {
85	        SSP1STAT = spi1_configuration[spiConfigIndex].stat;
86	        SSP1CON1 = spi1_configuration[spiConfigIndex].con1;
87	        SSP1CON3 = spi1_configuration[spiConfigIndex].con3;
88	        SSP1ADD  = spi1_configuration[spiConfigIndex].baud;
89	
90	        SSP1CON1bits.SSPEN = 1U;
91	
92	        returnValue = true;
93	    }
94	    else
95	    {
96	        returnValue = false;
97	    }
98	    return returnValue;
99	}

38	
39	/**
40	 * @ingroup crc
41	 * @brief Initializes the CRC module. This is called only once before calling other CRC APIs.
42	 * @param None.
43	 * @return None.
44	 */
45	void CRC_Initialize(void);
46	
47	/**
48	 * @ingroup crc
49	 * @brief Starts the CRC serial shifter.
50	 * @param None.
51	 * @return None.
52	 */
53	inline void CRC_StartCrc(void);
54	
55	/**
56	 * @ingroup crc
57	 * @brief Writes the data into the CRC Data registers.
58	 * @param data - CRC input data.
59	 * @retval True - Data write was successful.
60	 * @retval False - Data write was not successful.
61	 */
62	bool CRC_WriteData(uint32_t data);
63	
64	/**
65	 * @ingroup crc
66	 * @brief Returns the calculated CRC output value.
67	 * @param reverse - Determines if the normal (0) or reverse (1) value of the CRC output will be calculated.
68	 * @param xorValue - Value which is XOR-ed with CRC result.
69	 * @return CRC calculated result.
70	 */
71	uint32_t CRC_GetCalculatedResult(bool reverse, uint32_t xorValue);
72	
73	/**
74	 * @ingroup crc
75	 * @brief Returns the status of the CRCBUSY bit value to check if the shifting is in progress or not.
76	 * @param None.
77	 * @retval True - CRC bit shifting is in progress or pending.
78	 * @retval False - CRC bit shifting is complete or not pending.
79	 */
80	inline bool CRC_IsCrcBusy(void);
81	
82	/**
83	 * @ingroup crc
84	 * @brief Starts the scanning process.
85	 * @param None.
86	 * @return None.
87	 */
88	inline void CRC_StartCrcScanner(void);
89	
90	/**
91	 * @ingroup crc
92	 * @brief Stops the scanning process.
93	 * @param None.
94	 * @return None.
95	 */
96	inline void CRC_StopCrcScanner(void);
97	
98	/**
99	 * @ingroup crc
100	 * @brief Starts the CLB load process.
101	 * @param None.
102	 * @return None.
103	 */
104	inline void CRC_StartNvmScanner(void);
105	
106	/**
107	 * @ingroup crc
108	 * @brief Stopsthe CLB load process and switch back to CRC
109	 * @param None.
110	 * @return None.
111	 */
112	inline void CRC_StopNvmScanner(void);
113	
114	/**
115	 * @ingroup crc

## CODEBASE SEARCH REPORT: PIC16F13145 SPI-to-WS2812 MPLAB MCC Project

### PROJECT OVERVIEW
**Location:** `/c/microchip/mchp_fw/PIC16F13145_CNANO/firmware/pic16f13145-spi-ws2812-mplab-mcc/pic16f13145_spi_to_ws2812_mcc.X/`

### 1. UART/SERIAL COMMUNICATION SETUP AND FUNCTIONS

**UART Driver Files:**
- **Header:** `/mcc_generated_files/uart/eusart1.h`
- **Implementation:** `/mcc_generated_files/uart/src/eusart1.c`

**Key UART Functions:**

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `EUSART1_Initialize()` | Initialize EUSART1 with configured parameters (9600 baud, 8-bit, BRGL=0x44, BRGH=0x0) |
| `EUSART1_Enable()` / `EUSART1_Disable()` | Enable/disable UART module |
| `EUSART1_Read()` | Read byte from RX buffer |
| `EUSART1_Write(uint8_t txData)` | Write byte to TX buffer |
| `EUSART1_IsRxReady()` | Check if RX data available |
| `EUSART1_IsTxReady()` | Check if TX ready to accept data |
| `EUSART1_TransmitEnable()` / `EUSART1_TransmitDisable()` | TX control |
| `EUSART1_ReceiveEnable()` / `EUSART1_ReceiveDisable()` | RX control |
| `EUSART1_ReceiveISR()` | Receive Interrupt Service Routine |
| `EUSART1_TransmitISR()` | Transmit Interrupt Service Routine |

**Error Handling:**

- `EUSART1_FramingErrorCallbackRegister()` - Register framing error callback
- `EUSART1_OverrunErrorCallbackRegister()` - Register overrun error callback

**Interrupt Callbacks:**
- `EUSART1_RxCompleteCallbackRegister()` - Register RX complete callback
- `EUSART1_TxCompleteCallbackRegister()` - Register TX complete callback

### 2. CIRCULAR/RING BUFFER IMPLEMENTATION

**Found in:** `/mcc_generated_files/uart/src/eusart1.c` (lines 43-66)

**Buffer Configuration:**
```
TX Buffer Size: 8 bytes (2^n format)
TX Buffer Mask: 0x07 (for power-of-2 indexing)
RX Buffer Size: 8 bytes (2^n format)
RX Buffer Mask: 0x07
```

**Buffer Variables:**
- `eusart1TxHead` - TX write pointer
- `eusart1TxTail` - TX read pointer
- `eusart1TxBufferRemaining` - Available TX space
- `eusart1RxHead` - RX write pointer
- `eusart1RxTail` - RX read pointer
- `eusart1RxCount` - Bytes available in RX
- `eusart1RxStatusBuffer[8]` - Error status for each RX byte

**Buffer Mechanics:**
- Uses power-of-2 sizing with bit-mask (AND with BUFFER_MASK) for wrap-around
- Double-buffered with head/tail pointers
- RX overflow detection at line 305-308 (buffer full check)
- TX buffer remaining tracked to prevent overflow (line 330-338)

- **Implementation:** `/mcc_generated_files/system/src/interrupt.c`

  - Routes to `EUSART1_TxInterruptHandler()` or `EUSART1_RxInterruptHandler()`

**Interrupt Initialization:**
- `INTERRUPT_Initialize()` - Called from system initialization

1. `SYSTEM_Initialize()` - Initialize all system modules

3. Enable CLB (Configurable Logic Block): `CLBSWINLbits.CLBSWIN0 = 1`

6. Close SPI: `SPI1_Close()`
7. Infinite loop: `while(1);`

### KEY FINDINGS SUMMARY

1. **UART is fully implemented** with 8-byte circular buffers for both TX and RX, interrupt-driven operation, and error handling callbacks
2. **Ring buffers use power-of-2 sizing** (8 bytes) with bit-mask wrap-around for efficiency
3. **System uses centralized interrupt dispatcher** that routes TX1 and RC1 interrupts to UART ISR handlers
4. **Application is minimal** - just a single-shot LED update via SPI with no ongoing protocol handling
5. **No complex data parsing** is currently implemented - the application sends static LED patterns
6. **CLB module is used** for WS2812 timing (requires specific timing protocol for addressable LEDs)
7. **All modules follow MCC naming conventions** with Initialize/Enable/Disable/Read/Write/ISR patterns
8. **Callback-based architecture** allows runtime handler registration for UART error and completion events

<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```md
# Serial Protocol for WS2812 LED Control

## Context
The PIC16F13145 MCU has UART (EUSART1) fully implemented with interrupt-driven 8-byte circular buffers. Currently, the main.c sends LED data once at startup via SPI. The task is to create a serial protocol to allow a desktop GUI to control all 8 RGB LEDs in real-time via the Curiosity Nano's USB-UART interface. The communication is unidirectional (GUI → MCU).

## Protocol Design

### Frame Format (ASCII-based, human-readable)
\```
@<COMMAND>[<ARGS>]\n
\```

### Commands

#### 1. Set Individual LED
\```
@L<N>:<R>,<G>,<B>
\```
- `L` = LED command
- `N` = LED number (0-7)
- `R` = Red value (0-255)
- `G` = Green value (0-255)
- `B` = Blue value (0-255)
- Example: `@L0:255,0,0\n` (set LED 0 to red)

#### 2. Set All LEDs (Bulk)
\```
@A:<R>,<G>,<B>
\```
- Sets all 8 LEDs to the same color
- Example: `@A:50,50,50\n` (all white at 50 brightness)

#### 3. Clear/Off All LEDs
\```
@C
\```
- Sets all LEDs to off (0,0,0)
- Example: `@C\n`

#### 4. Update Display
\```
@U
\```
- Triggers immediate SPI transmission to LEDs
- Allows GUI to batch multiple LED commands before updating
- Example: `@L0:255,0,0\n@L1:0,255,0\n@U\n`

### Error Handling
- Invalid command format: silently ignored (no response in unidirectional mode)
- Out-of-range LED number (N > 7): silently ignored
- Out-of-range RGB values: clamped to 0-255
- Incomplete frame: held in buffer until newline received or timeout

## Implementation Files

### New Files to Create

#### 1. `led_protocol.h`
Contains:
- Protocol command byte definitions (@, L, A, C, U)
- Protocol parser state machine constants
- Function declarations:
  - `void LED_Protocol_Init(void)`
  - `void LED_Protocol_ProcessRxData(void)` - Called from main loop
  - `void LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs(void)` - Send current LED array via SPI
- Extern access to `rgb_t leds[NUMBER_OF_LEDS]`

#### 2. `led_protocol.c`
Contains:
- Command parser with state machine (looking for @, parsing command/args, waiting for \n)
- 64-byte command buffer for incoming frames
- `LED_Protocol_ProcessRxData()` implementation:
  - Read available UART bytes into command buffer
  - Parse complete commands (terminated with \n)
  - Validate and execute commands
  - Handle LED array updates
  - Auto-update SPI when commands modify LEDs
- Helper functions:
  - `parse_command()` - Tokenize and validate command format
  - `parse_uint8()` - Parse 0-255 integers from ASCII
  - Parse GRB format from serial to match WS2812 byte order

### Files to Modify

#### `main.c`
- Include `led_protocol.h`
- Call `LED_Protocol_Init()` after `SYSTEM_Initialize()`
- Add `LED_Protocol_ProcessRxData()` to main loop before the `while(1)`
- Initial LED display on startup (first `LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs()` call)
- Keep the existing `leds[]` array and SPI transmission logic

## Implementation Approach

1. **State Machine Pattern**: Command parser uses state machine to handle partial frames
   - STATE_IDLE: Waiting for @
   - STATE_CMD: Reading command character
   - STATE_ARGS: Reading arguments until \n
   - STATE_COMPLETE: Command ready to parse

2. **Minimal Buffering**: 64-byte command buffer sufficient for largest command:
   - Largest: `@L7:255,255,255\n` = ~16 bytes
   - Allows multiple commands to queue

3. **Auto-Update**: Each LED command immediately updates SPI
   - No explicit @U needed for single LED changes
   - @U useful for GUI batching multiple updates

4. **Error Tolerance**: Invalid commands ignored gracefully
   - No error responses (unidirectional protocol)
   - Partial frames cleared on buffer overflow or timeout

## Verification Plan

1. **Unit Test Protocol**
   - Send `@L0:255,0,0\n` → LED 0 should turn red
   - Send `@L1:0,255,0\n` → LED 1 should turn green
   - Send `@A:100,100,100\n` → All LEDs white at moderate brightness
   - Send `@C\n` → All LEDs off

2. **Integration Test**
   - Verify UART reception doesn't block SPI transmission
   - Test multiple rapid commands
   - Verify buffer doesn't overflow on spam

3. **Visual Verification on Hardware**
   - Connect Curiosity Nano to PC
   - Use serial terminal or GUI to send commands
   - Observe LED changes in real-time

## Technical Notes

- UART is interrupt-driven with 8-byte HW buffers; our parser reads in main loop with `EUSART1_IsRxReady()` and `EUSART1_Read()`
- SPI interface already works via `SPI1_Open()`, `SPI1_BufferWrite()`, `SPI1_Close()`
- LEDs are GRB format (Green byte, Red byte, Blue byte) - order critical for WS2812
- 9600 baud is standard for Curiosity Nano debug UART

```

<summary>Tool use: **AskUserQuestion**</summary>
**The protocol design uses ASCII format (@L0:255,0,0\n for individual LED, @A:255,0,0\n for all LEDs). Would you prefer a different approach, such as binary frames for compactness, or does ASCII suit your needs?**

Options:
- **ASCII (human-readable, easier debug)** - Keep the proposed ASCII format with text commands and decimal RGB values
- **Binary (compact, efficient)** - Use fixed-length binary frames: [START_BYTE][CMD][LED_NUM][R][G][B][CHECKSUM][END] for all LEDs
- **Bulk ASCII (all 24 bytes at once)** - Single command sends all 8 LEDs: @B:R0,G0,B0,R1,G1,B1,... (all 24 bytes in one frame)

**Should the MCU send acknowledgment responses back to the GUI (e.g., 'OK', 'ERR'), or keep it truly unidirectional with no responses?**

Options:
- **No responses (truly unidirectional)** - MCU silently processes all commands, no feedback to GUI
- **Simple ACK on success** - MCU responds with '+' on successful command, '-' on error
- **Status responses** - MCU sends current LED state periodically or on request (requires bidirectional)

</details></tool-use>

7

10	<COMMAND> [<ARGS>]\n

13	
14	### Commands
15	
16	#### 1. Set Individual LED
17	```
18	L<N> <R> <G> <B>
19	```
20	- `L` = LED command character
21	- `N` = LED number (0-7)
22	- `R` = Red value (0-255, space-separated)

24	- `B` = Blue value (0-255, space-separated)
25	- Example: `L0 255 0 0` (set LED 0 to red)
26	- Example: `L7 0 0 255` (set LED 7 to blue)
27	
28	#### 2. Set All LEDs (Bulk)
29	```

47	```

50	- Allows GUI/terminal to batch multiple LED commands before updating
51	- Example: `L0 255 0 0\nL1 0 255 0\nU` sends 3 commands then updates display
52	
53	### Error Handling
54	- Invalid command format: silently ignored (no response in unidirectional mode)
55	- Out-of-range LED number (N > 7): silently ignored
56	- Out-of-range RGB values: clamped to 0-255
57	- Incomplete frame: held in buffer until newline received or timeout
58	- Whitespace tolerance: multiple spaces between arguments handled gracefully

96	- Keep the existing `leds[]` array and SPI transmission logic

99	
100	1. **State Machine Pattern**: Command parser uses state machine to handle partial frames
101	   - STATE_IDLE: Waiting for @
102	   - STATE_CMD: Reading command character
103	   - STATE_ARGS: Reading arguments until \n
104	   - STATE_COMPLETE: Command ready to parse
105	
106	2. **Minimal Buffering**: 64-byte command buffer sufficient for largest command:
107	   - Largest: `@L7:255,255,255\n` = ~16 bytes
108	   - Allows multiple commands to queue

111	   - No explicit @U needed for single LED changes
112	   - @U useful for GUI batching multiple updates

## Approved Plan (edited by user):
# Serial Protocol for WS2812 LED Control

## Context
The PIC16F13145 MCU has UART (EUSART1) fully implemented with interrupt-driven 8-byte circular buffers. Currently, the main.c sends LED data once at startup via SPI. The task is to create a serial protocol to allow a desktop GUI to control all 8 RGB LEDs in real-time via the Curiosity Nano's USB-UART interface. The communication is unidirectional (GUI → MCU).

## Protocol Design

### Frame Format (Space-separated ASCII, terminal-friendly)
```
<COMMAND> [<ARGS>]\n
```
Single character commands followed by optional space-separated arguments.

### Commands

#### 1. Set Individual LED
```
L<N> <R> <G> <B>
```
- `L` = LED command character
- `N` = LED number (0-7)
- `R` = Red value (0-255, space-separated)
- `G` = Green value (0-255, space-separated)
- `B` = Blue value (0-255, space-separated)
- Example: `L0 255 0 0` (set LED 0 to red)
- Example: `L7 0 0 255` (set LED 7 to blue)

#### 2. Set All LEDs (Bulk)
```
A <R> <G> <B>
```
- `A` = All LEDs command
- Sets all 8 LEDs to the same color
- Example: `A 50 50 50` (all white at 50 brightness)

#### 3. Clear/Off All LEDs
```
C
```
- Single character command, no arguments
- Sets all LEDs to off (0,0,0)
- Example: `C`

#### 4. Update Display
```
U
```
- Single character command, no arguments
- Triggers immediate SPI transmission to LEDs
- Allows GUI/terminal to batch multiple LED commands before updating
- Example: `L0 255 0 0\nL1 0 255 0\nU` sends 3 commands then updates display

### Error Handling
- Invalid command format: silently ignored (no response in unidirectional mode)
- Out-of-range LED number (N > 7): silently ignored
- Out-of-range RGB values: clamped to 0-255
- Incomplete frame: held in buffer until newline received or timeout
- Whitespace tolerance: multiple spaces between arguments handled gracefully

## Implementation Files

### New Files to Create

#### 1. `led_protocol.h`
Contains:
- Protocol command character definitions (L, A, C, U)
- Buffer and parser constants
- Function declarations:
  - `void LED_Protocol_Init(void)`
  - `void LED_Protocol_ProcessRxData(void)` - Called from main loop
  - `void LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs(void)` - Send current LED array via SPI
- Extern access to `rgb_t leds[NUMBER_OF_LEDS]`

#### 2. `led_protocol.c`
Contains:
- Command parser with line-based tokenization (simple whitespace splitting)
- 64-byte command buffer for incoming frames
- `LED_Protocol_ProcessRxData()` implementation:
  - Read available UART bytes into command buffer
  - Parse complete commands (terminated with \n)
  - Tokenize by whitespace (space/tab)
  - Validate command letter (L, A, C, U) and argument count
  - Execute commands and clamp/validate ranges
  - Handle LED array updates
  - Auto-update SPI when commands modify LEDs
- Helper functions:
  - `tokenize_command()` - Split command line by whitespace into tokens
  - `parse_uint8()` - Parse 0-255 integers from ASCII strings
  - Handle RGB input order (R, G, B from serial) to WS2812 byte order (G, R, B in leds array)

### Files to Modify

#### `main.c`
- Include `led_protocol.h`
- Call `LED_Protocol_Init()` after `SYSTEM_Initialize()`
- Add `LED_Protocol_ProcessRxData()` to main loop before the `while(1)`
- Initial LED display on startup (first `LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs()` call)
- Keep the existing `leds[]` array and SPI transmission logic

## Implementation Approach

1. **Simple Line-Based Parser**: No state machine needed
   - Read bytes into 64-byte buffer until \n received
   - On newline, tokenize by whitespace
   - Parse first token as command letter (L, A, C, U)
   - Parse remaining tokens as arguments
   - Clamp out-of-range values, ignore invalid commands

2. **Minimal Buffering**: 64-byte command buffer sufficient for largest command:
   - Largest: `L7 255 255 255\n` = ~16 bytes
   - Simple buffer management: add at head, clear on \n

3. **Auto-Update on Command**: Each LED command immediately updates SPI
   - Command L and A trigger `LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs()` 
   - Command U explicitly triggers update (for batch operations)
   - Command C clears array and updates

4. **Error Tolerance**: Invalid commands ignored gracefully
   - No error responses (unidirectional protocol)
   - Partial frames held until \n or buffer full

## Verification Plan

1. **Unit Test Protocol in Terminal**
   - Send `L0 255 0 0` → LED 0 should turn red
   - Send `L1 0 255 0` → LED 1 should turn green
   - Send `A 100 100 100` → All LEDs white at moderate brightness
   - Send `C` → All LEDs off
   - Send `L0 255 0 0\nL1 0 255 0\nU` → Batch update multiple LEDs then transmit once

2. **Integration Test**
   - Verify UART reception doesn't block SPI transmission
   - Test multiple rapid commands
   - Verify buffer doesn't overflow on spam
   - Test with various whitespace patterns

3. **Visual Verification on Hardware**
   - Connect Curiosity Nano to PC via USB
   - Open serial terminal (PuTTY, minicom, Arduino IDE Serial Monitor, etc.)
   - Type commands directly and press Enter
   - Observe LED changes in real-time

## Technical Notes

- UART is interrupt-driven with 8-byte HW buffers; our parser reads in main loop with `EUSART1_IsRxReady()` and `EUSART1_Read()`
- SPI interface already works via `SPI1_Open()`, `SPI1_BufferWrite()`, `SPI1_Close()`
- LEDs are GRB format (Green byte, Red byte, Blue byte) - order critical for WS2812
- 9600 baud is standard for Curiosity Nano debug UART
```
</details></tool-use>

#ifndef LED_PROTOCOL_H
#define LED_PROTOCOL_H

#include <stdint.h>

typedef struct
{
    uint8_t green;
    uint8_t red;
    uint8_t blue;
} rgb_t;

#define NUMBER_OF_LEDS  8
#define RGB_SIZE        (NUMBER_OF_LEDS * 3)

extern rgb_t leds[NUMBER_OF_LEDS];

void LED_Protocol_Init(void);
void LED_Protocol_ProcessRxData(void);
void LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs(void);

#endif

_**Agent (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 2026-05-06 20:04:21Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```c
#include "led_protocol.h"
#include "mcc_generated_files/uart/eusart1.h"
#include "mcc_generated_files/spi/mssp1.h"
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <ctype.h>

rgb_t leds[NUMBER_OF_LEDS];

static uint8_t cmd_buffer[64];
static uint8_t cmd_index = 0;

static uint8_t parse_uint8(const char *str)
{
    uint16_t val = atoi(str);
    if (val > 255)
        val = 255;
    return (uint8_t)val;
}

static void tokenize_and_execute(void)
{
    char *tokens[5];
    uint8_t token_count = 0;
    uint8_t i = 0;

    cmd_buffer[cmd_index] = '\0';

    char *copy = (char *)cmd_buffer;
    char *token = NULL;

    while (*copy && token_count < 5)
    {
        while (*copy && (isspace(*copy)))
            copy++;

        if (*copy == '\0')
            break;

        tokens[token_count] = copy;
        token_count++;

        while (*copy && !isspace(*copy))
            copy++;

        if (*copy)
            *copy++ = '\0';
    }

    if (token_count == 0)
        return;

    char cmd = tokens[0][0];

    switch (cmd)
    {
        case 'L':
        case 'l':
            if (token_count >= 5)
            {
                uint8_t led_num = parse_uint8(tokens[0] + 1);
                if (led_num >= NUMBER_OF_LEDS)
                    break;

                uint8_t r = parse_uint8(tokens[1]);
                uint8_t g = parse_uint8(tokens[2]);
                uint8_t b = parse_uint8(tokens[3]);

                leds[led_num].green = g;
                leds[led_num].red = r;
                leds[led_num].blue = b;

                LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs();
            }
            break;

        case 'A':
        case 'a':
            if (token_count >= 4)
            {
                uint8_t r = parse_uint8(tokens[1]);
                uint8_t g = parse_uint8(tokens[2]);
                uint8_t b = parse_uint8(tokens[3]);

                for (uint8_t i = 0; i < NUMBER_OF_LEDS; i++)
                {
                    leds[i].green = g;
                    leds[i].red = r;
                    leds[i].blue = b;
                }

                LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs();
            }
            break;

        case 'C':
        case 'c':
            for (uint8_t i = 0; i < NUMBER_OF_LEDS; i++)
            {
                leds[i].green = 0;
                leds[i].red = 0;
                leds[i].blue = 0;
            }
            LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs();
            break;

        case 'U':
        case 'u':
            LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs();
            break;

        default:
            break;
    }
}

void LED_Protocol_Init(void)
{
    cmd_index = 0;
    memset(cmd_buffer, 0, sizeof(cmd_buffer));
    EUSART1_Initialize();
}

void LED_Protocol_ProcessRxData(void)
{
    while (EUSART1_IsRxReady())
    {
        uint8_t ch = EUSART1_Read();

        if (ch == '\n' || ch == '\r')
        {
            if (cmd_index > 0)
            {
                tokenize_and_execute();
                cmd_index = 0;
            }
        }
        else if (cmd_index < (sizeof(cmd_buffer) - 1))
        {
            cmd_buffer[cmd_index++] = ch;
        }
    }
}

void LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs(void)
{
    SPI1_Open(MSSP1_DEFAULT);
    SPI1_BufferWrite((uint8_t *)leds, RGB_SIZE);
    SPI1_Close();
}

```

```

117	void LED_Protocol_Init(void)
118	{
119	    cmd_index = 0;
120	    memset(cmd_buffer, 0, sizeof(cmd_buffer));
121	    EUSART1_Initialize();
122	}
123	
124	void LED_Protocol_ProcessRxData(void)
125	{
126	    while (EUSART1_IsRxReady())
127	    {
128	        uint8_t ch = EUSART1_Read();
129	
130	        if (ch == '\n' || ch == '\r')
131	        {
132	            if (cmd_index > 0)
133	            {
134	                tokenize_and_execute();
135	                cmd_index = 0;
136	            }
137	        }
138	        else if (cmd_index < (sizeof(cmd_buffer) - 1))
139	        {
140	            cmd_buffer[cmd_index++] = ch;
141	        }
142	    }
143	}
144	
145	void LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs(void)
146	{
147	    SPI1_Open(MSSP1_DEFAULT);
148	    SPI1_BufferWrite((uint8_t *)leds, RGB_SIZE);
149	    SPI1_Close();
150	}

_**Agent (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 2026-05-06 20:04:53Z)**_

Done! I've implemented the ASCII serial protocol for LED control. Here's what was created:

## New Files

**`led_protocol.h`** — Header with data structures and function declarations:
- `rgb_t` struct (GREEN, RED, BLUE byte order for WS2812)
- `LED_Protocol_Init()` — Initializes UART
- `LED_Protocol_ProcessRxData()` — Called in main loop, processes incoming commands
- `LED_Protocol_UpdateLEDs()` — Sends LED array to hardware via SPI

**`led_protocol.c`** — Protocol implementation:
- 64-byte command buffer with line-based parsing
- Tokenizes by whitespace (handles multiple spaces)
- Parses commands: `L`, `A`, `C`, `U` (both upper/lowercase)
- Clamps RGB values to 0-255 range
- Case-insensitive, error-tolerant

## Modified Files

**`main.c`** — Integrated protocol:
- Calls `LED_Protocol_Init()` after system init
- Initializes all LEDs to dim white (0x0D each channel = 5% brightness)
- Loops calling `LED_Protocol_ProcessRxData()` to handle incoming serial commands

## Protocol Usage (Terminal-Ready)

Type directly into serial terminal:
```
L0 255 0 0      → Set LED 0 to red
L1 0 255 0      → Set LED 1 to green
A 50 50 50      → All LEDs to white at 50 brightness
C               → Clear all (off)
U               → Update display (for batch operations)
```

Ready to compile and test!
